title: Passbolt vs TeamPassword for Power users
meta_description: Passbolt vs TeamPassword for power users who want self hosted credential infrastructure. See why Passbolt wins when server control matters.
constraint_lens: Power users prefer tools that can be deployed and controlled inside their own infrastructure instead of relying on hosted services.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users who want to run password sharing infrastructure on their own servers.
one_second_verdict.reason: TeamPassword fails first because it cannot be deployed and maintained on a self hosted server environment.

verdict.summary: Passbolt is the better choice for power users who want full control over their credential sharing system. It can be deployed on a self hosted server where administrators manage the infrastructure directly. TeamPassword operates as a hosted service where credentials live inside the provider platform. For organizations that want password management integrated into their own server environment, relying on a hosted service limits control.
verdict.decision_rule: If the password manager cannot be deployed and maintained on a self-hosted server environment, TeamPassword fails first.

sections.heading: Why Passbolt fits power users
sections.content: The user runs infrastructure on their own servers and wants credential management integrated into that environment. Passbolt can be installed on a private server and managed directly by the organization. Administrators control updates, storage, and security policies. This allows password sharing to operate inside the same infrastructure used by other internal services.

bullets.point: Passbolt can be deployed on a self hosted server controlled by the organization.
bullets.why_it_matters: Teams can integrate credential management directly into their own infrastructure.

bullets.point: Passbolt stores shared credentials inside the self managed server environment.
bullets.why_it_matters: Organizations maintain full control of where password data resides.

bullets.point: Passbolt allows administrators to configure server level access and security policies.
bullets.why_it_matters: Power users can align password management with internal infrastructure standards.

bullets.point: TeamPassword runs as a hosted service without requiring server deployment.
bullets.why_it_matters: Teams can start sharing credentials without installing infrastructure.

bullets.point: TeamPassword provides shared vault permissions through a managed platform.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users can assign access to credentials without managing server configuration.

bullets.point: TeamPassword maintains the underlying infrastructure and updates automatically.
bullets.why_it_matters: Teams avoid managing server maintenance tasks.

items.fails_when: The team does not want to install or maintain server infrastructure.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use TeamPassword where the service is hosted and managed automatically.

items.fails_when: The organization requires full infrastructure control because TeamPassword cannot be deployed on a private server.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Passbolt where the password manager can run inside a self hosted environment.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If the organization prefers a hosted password manager and does not want to maintain server infrastructure, TeamPassword may become the better option.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Pick Passbolt if you want password management running on your own servers.
Pick Passbolt if infrastructure control and internal deployment matter.
Pick TeamPassword if you want a hosted password sharing service without server maintenance.

faqs.q: Why do power users choose Passbolt?
faqs.a: Passbolt can be deployed on a self hosted server so organizations control their credential infrastructure.
No. TeamPassword operates as a hosted service and cannot be deployed on private servers.

faqs.q: Does Passbolt support team credential sharing?
faqs.a: Yes. Passbolt provides permission based access for sharing credentials across teams.
Teams that prefer a managed hosted password sharing service may prefer TeamPassword.
